title: Polymer clay geometric pendant
subtitle: A clean angular shape baked in a domestic oven and strung on cord
excerpt: A flat geometric pendant cut from a conditioned polymer clay sheet and baked in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Shapes include hexagons, triangles, and elongated diamonds. No kiln needed. The cured piece threads onto a cord or chain for wearing.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay forming technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Polymer clay jewellery starts with a flat sheet and a clean geometric cut. The baked result is hard, lightweight, and takes acrylic paint or mica powder well. This tutorial covers the basic pendant workflow: condition, roll, cut, pierce, bake, finish.
Polymer clay
is decorative only; not for food contact.

attrs.body: Use a dedicated oven liner or tray. Do not share the baking surface with food-prep cookware. Verify the oven temperature with a thermometer; polymer clay burns above 175°C.
attrs.title: Baking polymer clay

content.text: Making the pendant
1. Condition the clay

Break the polymer clay block into small pieces and work them in the hands for two to three minutes until soft and pliable with no cracks when folded. Polymer clay fresh from a cold shop may need warming in the palm for a few minutes first. Well-conditioned clay rolls to a smooth flat sheet without crumbling at the edges.
2. Roll a flat sheet

Roll the conditioned clay to a flat sheet 3 mm thick between two pieces of greaseproof paper. Polymer clay sticks to bare wood rolling pins; the paper prevents transfer. A 57 g block produces a sheet roughly 12 cm by 10 cm at this thickness.
3. Cut the shape

Cut the pendant shape from the rolled sheet. A hexagon cut from a 4 cm diameter circle suits this thickness. A triangle cut to 4 cm sides produces a clean geometric form. An elongated diamond 6 cm by 2 cm works as an earring pair.
4. Pierce the cord hole

Pierce a hole 3 mm from the top edge using a needle tool or fine skewer. The hole should be at least 2 mm in diameter; cord or a jump ring needs to pass through cleanly. Twist the needle gently rather than pushing straight through, which reduces tear-out on the far side.
5. Bake

Place on the dedicated baking tray. Bake at 110°C (fan) for 20-25 minutes. Check the manufacturer's temperature for the specific polymer clay you are using and take the lower figure if in doubt. The pendant is
curing
rather than firing: it hardens as the PVC sets. Allow to cool fully on the tray before handling: the cured piece is fragile when hot.
Finishing

Once cooled, sand any rough edges with 400-grit then 800-grit sandpaper. Leave the surface as a matte finish or buff with a cloth to a slight sheen. Paint with acrylics, rub metallic mica powder onto the surface before baking, or leave the clay's natural colour. Thread onto a waxed cotton cord or attach to a chain via a jump ring.
Care
Polymer clay jewellery is robust once cured but brittle if dropped on hard surfaces. Do not expose to direct sun for prolonged periods; some colours fade. Do not wear in the shower or swimming.
Variations
Marble two colours together before rolling to produce a marbled sheet. Roll until the blend is just integrated; over-working produces a flat mixed colour.
Press a texture stamp into the surface before baking for a repeating pattern relief.

items.fix: Warm in the palms for five minutes; very old clay may be too stiff to condition; discard if it crumbles with no pliability
items.cause: Clay is too cold or old stock
items.symptom: Clay cracks when folded during conditioning

items.fix: Verify oven temperature with a thermometer; reduce to 110°C and limit to 25 minutes
items.cause: Oven temperature too high or baked too long

items.fix: Place the hole at least 3 mm in from the edge; reinforce by pressing a tiny ring of clay around the hole before baking
items.cause: Hole too close to the top edge
items.symptom: Cord hole tears at the edge
